projects.title: Analog IC Studio — Automated Analog IC Tuning Platform
projects.description: A Python + Ngspice + SciPy platform that automates analog IC design: enter spec targets and the optimizer closes the loop in simulation to back-solve each transistor's W/L.

detail.purpose:
Analog IC design traditionally relies on manual, trial-and-error tuning. This platform automates it: the user enters spec targets (e.g. 60dB gain, 3GHz oscillation, minimal temperature drift), and the system uses real Ngspice circuit simulation plus SciPy multi-variable optimization to back-solve every MOSFET's width W and length L, emitting device parameters that map one-to-one back into KiCad.

It covers six circuit topologies (two-stage OPA, bandgap reference, ring oscillator, current-starved VCO, LC-VCO) and offers a fast Level-1 model alongside the real SkyWater sky130 PDK, balancing iteration speed against process fidelity.
detail.concept:
[Simulation engine] Ngspice at the core: AC/DC/transient analyses auto-extract Gain/PM/UGF, temperature coefficient, oscillation frequency, backed by the SkyWater sky130 open PDK (BSIM4 real process models with tt/ff/ss corners). A self-healing guard detects non-convergence and retries, distinguishing flaky crashes from deterministic dead zones.

[Optimization] Each transistor gets its own W/L (up to 14 dimensions); SciPy Differential Evolution for global search plus Nelder-Mead for local refinement, evaluated in parallel across threads. A physics guard enforces Wp = 2-3x Wn (electron/hole mobility ratio) to reject non-physical solutions.

[RFIC & yield] Phase noise (Leeson estimate), FTR tuning curve and Kvco extraction, Monte-Carlo yield with a Pelgrom mismatch model (sigma proportional to 1/sqrt(WL), so larger devices match better), 27-corner PVT scan, Pareto multi-objective packages, and layout finger splitting (single finger <= 10um).
detail.outcome:
All six topologies were fully parameterized to per-instance W/L, eliminating black boxes. Auto-optimization reaches Gain 65dB / PM 63° for the OPA; the bandgap trims to 2 ppm/°C; the LC-VCO achieves phase-noise FoM -186 dBc/Hz (~18dB better than the ring, concretely demonstrating the LC-vs-ring phase-noise/tuning-range trade-off).

The frontend is an Apple-style single-page dashboard (Chinese UI, English chart labels) with a live optimization progress bar; deployed via Docker to Hugging Face Spaces (apt ngspice + Flask) for a permanent, publicly runnable demo.

Engineering-wise, 13 physics/engineering defects were systematically audited and fixed (fake yield model, phase wrapping, device self-loading, fast-vs-accurate fidelity inversion, etc.), with simplification boundaries honestly labeled (phase noise is an analytical estimate; the LC inductor is idealized) rather than overstating the simulation's capability.

projects.title: ICU VAP Prediction: Pilot Methodology Validation
projects.description: Pilot methodology validation on a single-center ICU dataset (Far Eastern Memorial Hospital, n=109). Core contribution: designed Stay-Level 5-Fold Stratified CV to identify and correct patient-level data leakage in the PREDICT 2025 baseline (AUROC inflated 0.99 → true 0.58). Integrated Gradients eliminated co-linear redundancy (mv_total where mv≡Vt×RR), distilling 14 features to 4 non-invasive metrics. Scope: single-center pilot — rigorous methodology validation, not a clinical deployment product.

images.caption: Pipeline: feature selection → sliding window → LSTM → multi-horizon evaluation
Fig. 1  LSTM vs. traditional ML baselines (AUROC / AUPRC / Sensitivity / Specificity)
Fig. 2  LSTM 4-feat performance across warning horizons (6–72 h)

detail.purpose:
VAP incidence is ~5–15% in mechanically ventilated patients with 20–50% mortality, and clinical diagnosis typically lags 48+ hours behind onset. Current methods rely on periodic assessment and cannot leverage the temporal trends available from continuous monitoring.

Using Far Eastern Memorial Hospital ICU data (2019–2023, n=109: VAP 54, non-VAP 55), we built an LSTM time-series classification pipeline using 4 routine vital signs (MAP, respiratory rate, tidal volume, SpO₂) to provide 6–72 hour early warning of VAP.

Model selection — LSTM vs. GRU/Transformer:
VAP physiological changes exhibit continuous temporal drift (visible 48–72 hours before infection onset), so static features are insufficient to capture long-range dependencies. LSTM cell state shows better gradient stability than GRU on 72-hour windows; Transformer overfits on the n=109 small dataset. LSTM achieved consistently higher AUROC/AUPRC than LR, RF, and SVM across all windows.

[Study Limitations & Honest Scope]
This project is a pilot methodology validation, not a production clinical system. Key constraints: (1) Single-center dataset from Far Eastern Memorial Hospital only — generalizability to other ICU populations is not yet established. (2) Small sample size (n=109) — results demonstrate the effectiveness of Stay-Level CV as a validation methodology; they should not be interpreted as evidence of deployment-ready clinical performance. (3) External validation on a multi-center, larger dataset is required before any clinical deployment consideration. The high AUROC on local data reflects a well-controlled, leakage-free experimental design applied to a small homogeneous cohort.
detail.concept:
[1. Paper Replication & Data Leakage Discovery]
Based on the PREDICT paper (JCM 2025), we fully replicated its 3-layer LSTM architecture using the MIMIC-IV public dataset. During replication, we discovered that the original paper used Window-level Cross Validation — the same patient's time windows appeared in both training and test sets, inflating AUROC to 0.99. This data leakage finding is one of the core contributions of this research.

[2. Correct Validation Methodology]
Switching to Stay-level Cross Validation (patient-wise splitting) eliminated the leakage and revealed true performance (AUROC ≈ 0.58) on MIMIC-IV, confirming the limitations of the original paper's results.

For the local Far Eastern ICU dataset (2019–2023, 110 enrolled, 1 excluded for <72h ventilation, effective n=109: VAP 54, non-VAP 55; VAP group had significantly higher APACHE II on admission: 25.9 vs 20.1, p<0.001), SMOTE (1:10) was applied within each training fold only, ensuring oversampling did not cross train/test boundaries.

[3. Feature Engineering & Interpretability]
14 physiological and ventilation features were analyzed with Integrated Gradients (IG), yielding the top 4 most predictive features: MAP (mean arterial pressure), Vent Rate (ventilator set rate), Tidal Volume, SpO₂. Ablation studies further validated the indispensability of each.

[4. Technical Architecture]
- Model: 3-layer LSTM (50 units each) + Dropout, binary classification output
  (Depth rationale: 2 layers show gradient vanishing on 72h windows; 4 layers overfit on small samples; 3 layers is optimal)
- Input: 24-hour sliding window × 4 features, sliding by 1 hour
- Framework: PyTorch + GPU (NVIDIA RTX 4080 Laptop)
- Interpretability: Integrated Gradients
- Baselines: Logistic Regression, Random Forest, SVM (same settings, fair comparison)
detail.outcome:
[Model Performance — Far Eastern ICU, Stay-level 5-fold CV]

Horizon      AUROC    AUPRC    Sensitivity  Specificity
6 hours      0.9800   0.8136   78.95%       99.83%
12 hours     0.9817   0.8565   81.06%       99.71%
24 hours     0.9919   0.9325   88.72%       99.64%
48 hours     0.9908   0.9352   87.46%       99.18%
72 hours     0.9865   0.9210   86.34%       98.67%

The model achieves its best balance at the 24-hour prediction window (AUROC=0.99), alerting clinicians one full day in advance. LSTM significantly outperforms LR, RF, and SVM across all windows.

[Core Features — Top 4, IG Analysis]
- MAP (Mean Arterial Pressure)
- Vent Rate (Ventilator Set Rate)
- Tidal Volume
- SpO₂ (Oxygen Saturation)

[Research Contributions]
- Taiwan-localized data: first VAP prediction model validated on real Taiwan ICU data
- Extended prediction window: beyond the 24h literature limit, achieving 72h early warning
- Rigorous validation: Stay-level CV + per-fold normalization to prevent data leakage
- Lightweight feature set: only 4 routine monitoring indicators, no invasive procedures
- Clinical interpretability: IG analysis surfaces model reasoning to build physician trust

[Ablation Study: mv_total exclusion]
mv_total (minute ventilation) ranked #1 in IG attribution (0.00989) but is mathematically redundant (mv≡Vt×RR). Removing it actually improved 24h AUROC by +0.004 and AUPRC by +0.014, confirming that feature importance should be validated by model behavior, not attribution scores alone.

📄 Paper: "LSTM-Based Early Prediction of Ventilator-Associated Pneumonia Using Stay-Level Cross-Validation and Extended Prediction Windows"
Manuscript submitted to IEEE GCCE 2026 (2026.05)

projects.title: AWS × BitoPro Hackathon: BitOGuard
projects.description: XGBoost/LightGBM × S3→Glue→Athena→SageMaker→Lambda→Bedrock end-to-end AML pipeline; 63,770 accounts / 413,235 transactions; 31 behavioral features (incl. graph-theory blacklist neighbors) + SHAP compliance reports to identify money mule accounts. Streamlit dashboard live.

images.caption: Dashboard overview: AUC 83.2%, Precision 27.5%, Recall 33.2%, confusion matrix + 5-fold CV bar chart
Account risk query: USER ID 971465 scored 82.1% — classified as extreme high risk, blacklisted
Risk ranking: Top 50 high-risk (CRITICAL) vs. Top 50 low-risk accounts
Model evaluation: feature importance (total_volume highest) + decision threshold analysis (optimal 0.24)
Predictions: 12,753 users — 501 flagged as risk (3.93%), 12,252 normal (96.07%)

detail.purpose:
BitoPro cryptocurrency exchange AML compliance competition dataset:
- User records: 63,770 accounts
- TWD transfer records: 195,601 (56,111 users)
- USDT transaction records: 217,634 (33,587 users)
- Labeled money mule accounts: 1,640 (3.21% of training set)

Goal: output a risk score per account to identify high-risk suspicious accounts while satisfying regulators' demand for explainability in AML compliance (black-box outputs not accepted).

Why XGBoost over deep learning:
AML compliance requires feature-level justification for every flagged account. XGBoost + SHAP directly outputs each feature's contribution per account, satisfying compliance reporting obligations. On <65K structured data, XGBoost also trains faster and is more stable than neural networks.
detail.concept:
[Why XGBoost, Not Deep Learning?]
AML compliance systems require explainability: regulators demand "which features triggered high risk" for every flagged account. XGBoost with SHAP values directly outputs per-feature contributions for individual accounts, meeting compliance reporting requirements. On this scale of structured transactional data (<65K accounts), XGBoost also trains faster with more stable hyperparameters and natively supports scale_pos_weight to handle 1:30 class imbalance.

[Data Pipeline — AWS End-to-End Architecture]
① BitoPro API ingestion → ingest_to_s3.py → AWS S3 (raw data layer)
② AWS Glue ETL + glue_graph_hops.py for graph-based multi-hop association analysis
③ Amazon Athena SQL queries to aggregate feature results (athena_graph_hops.sql)
④ Amazon SageMaker for XGBoost model training, hyperparameter tuning, and deployment
⑤ AWS Lambda + lambda_diagnosis.py for real-time inference and event-driven logic
⑥ Amazon Bedrock integrates SHAP + XAI to auto-generate explainability reports per high-risk account
⑦ Streamlit dashboard (app.py) for interactive risk review
⑧ CloudFormation template.yaml defines full IaC

[Feature Engineering — 31 Behavioral Indicators]
Competition version started with 3 core features (TWD deposit/withdrawal count, late-night transaction ratio), expanded post-competition to 31 indicators:
- Transaction behavior: total_volume, tx_per_day, avg_twd_amount, swap_twd_volume
- Temporal features: min_retention_minutes, night_tx_ratio
- Graph features: blacklist_neighbor_count, is_direct_neighbor
- Risk flags: high_speed_risk, ip_anomaly, deposit_only_flag
Top 3 by importance: total_volume, account age, swap_twd_volume

[Model Design]
- XGBoost (max_depth=8, lr=0.05, 1000 rounds, early stopping=30)
- 5-fold Stratified CV with consistent positive/negative ratios per fold
- scale_pos_weight + threshold sweep (0.3–0.9) to mitigate 1:30 imbalance
- SHAP analysis shows per-feature contribution for individual accounts
detail.outcome:
[Competition Result]
- Completed AWS × BitoPro Hackathon; end-to-end cloud architecture received positive feedback from judges
- 81 commits, fully implementing the pipeline from API data ingestion to model prediction

[Technical Achievements]
- Successfully chained 6 AWS services: SageMaker, Glue, Athena, Lambda, S3, Bedrock
- Feature engineering expanded from 3 competition features to 31 behavioral indicators
- SHAP interpretability module provides AML-compliant explanations
- Streamlit dashboard supports interactive account risk review — deployed live

[Model Performance — 5-fold Stratified CV]
- Precision: 9.83% (±8.12%)
- Recall: 0.43% (±0.36%)
- F1 Score: 0.82%
Competition submission used a conservative threshold (0.65), flagging only 2 high-risk accounts — intentionally biased toward precision to avoid false positives against legitimate users.

[Honest Technical Reflection: Why These Metrics Are a Reasonable Starting Point]
Low recall directly reflects AML's triple challenge: ① only 3.21% labeled samples (1:30 imbalance); ② dataset lacks high-discriminating features like device fingerprints and IP associations; ③ money mule behavior patterns heavily overlap with legitimate high-frequency traders. Real financial AML systems face the same fundamental Precision vs. Recall tradeoff. Future improvements: graph neural networks (GNN) for blacklist neighbor relationships, SMOTE/ADASYN for imbalance, expanded account age and IP-sharing features.

projects.title: Job Radar: Personalized Job Search Dashboard
projects.description: A GitHub Actions cron job scrapes ~49 target companies daily from three sources (104, Cake, LinkedIn — about 1,300 listings/day), scores and ranks them against my own criteria, and builds a static dashboard deployed on Cloudflare Pages. No backend, no database — data processing and rendering are fully separated.

detail.purpose:
Most job boards rank listings by general popularity, which has nothing to do with whether a role actually fits me. I wanted a system centered on my own skills and constraints: track the companies I'm targeting, rank them by weights I set myself, and separate "what I can apply to as a student now" from "what's only open after graduation."

So I built a tool that runs itself every morning at 8:30 without needing my computer on.
detail.concept:
Data and presentation are separated: the scraper produces structured JSON, the build step statically renders cards into a single HTML file, and the frontend JS only handles filtering, search, and saved/applied status (stored in localStorage). No backend, no database — just static files on a CDN, fast and cheap.

Each source needs a different scraping approach: 104 uses a reverse-engineered company-page API (pulling "all listings" instead of keyword search, to avoid missing postings); Cake's job pages are JS-rendered, so I use Playwright headless; LinkedIn uses its public guest job endpoint over plain HTTP, no login needed. The three sources have different formats, so I normalize them into one card schema before merging and ranking.

Scoring weights (company fit, title keyword matches, commute zone, experience requirements, junior-friendliness) all live in config.json so I can tune them without touching code. Listings are also bucketed by my graduation timeline into "apply now / campus recruiting targets / post-graduation pool" so "a good fit" and "something I can apply to right now" don't get conflated.
detail.outcome:
It updates automatically every day, pulling roughly 1,300 listings across the three sources. A few real problems I had to fix along the way:

- 104's old joblist API was discontinued, so I reverse-engineered the company-page SPA to find the current endpoint
- LinkedIn blocks generic scrapers, so I switched to its public guest endpoint to get around the login wall
- It originally ran on a local Windows scheduled task, which meant nothing happened if my computer was off — moved it to GitHub Actions cron
- CI deploys started failing because the Cloudflare token had invisible characters in it; fixed by stripping non-printable characters with tr -cd and printing the string length to diagnose
- Telling "is this a new listing" apart required remembering what I'd seen across runs, solved by committing seen_jobs.json to version control

Personal academic data (graduation credits, application strategy) lives behind a login-protected page; the public job radar and rankings pages don't contain personal data.

projects.title: Analog Circuit Implementation: Audio Amplifier PCB Design
projects.description: Sophomore course project building foundational hardware literacy: schematic reading and interpretation, through-hole soldering, and basic PCB routing topology for a two-stage BJT audio amplifier (CE + CC). Completed the full photoresist-etching fabrication flow and hands-on systematic debugging — rebuilt twice, diagnosing a residual-copper short and a component pin mirror error, before successfully driving an 8Ω speaker.

images.caption: Final product: two-stage BJT amplifier board + 8Ω speaker, driving audio output
Photoresist-etched PCB blank: clear traces, ready for drilling and soldering
EDA schematic: CE amplifier stage + bias network + CC emitter follower
PCB component placement (top): 109.2×45.7mm, transistors, capacitors, trimpots
PCB component placement (bottom): confirming mirrored pin orientation — key lesson from two rebuilds
PCB copper traces: full routing for CE + CC stages
PCB drill pattern: all component pads and mounting hole machining reference

detail.purpose:
Sophomore Electronics final project (Group 12): design and implement an audio amplifier circuit capable of driving an 8Ω speaker. The circuit needed adjustable gain and required completing the full flow from circuit simulation and breadboard verification to actual PCB fabrication.

Personal responsibilities: full PCB flow — schematic drawing, layout design, photoresist board fabrication (exposure, development, acid etching), drilling, soldering, and debugging.
detail.concept:
[Circuit Topology: Two-Stage BJT Amplification]
- Input stage: trimpots Rv1a/Rv1b adjust input gain; R3/R4 DC bias stabilizes Q1 in active region
- First stage — CE (Common Emitter): Q1 provides main voltage gain; collector resistor RC converts current changes to output voltage
- Bias stabilization: D1–D4 (4 series diodes) stabilize base voltage for Q2/Q3; C3 absorbs reverse voltage spikes to prevent diode breakdown
- Second stage — CC (Common Collector / Emitter Follower): high input impedance, low output impedance; emitter resistor RE provides negative feedback for stability and low distortion, voltage gain ≈1
- Trimpot Rv2 adjusts overall DC operating point to find maximum gain without distortion

[PCB Fabrication Flow]
Photosensitive FR4 double-sided board (15cm × 12.5cm), hand-fabricated:
① Layout → minimize white-line crossings, compress routing under components
② Film output → exposure → development → acid etching → rinse
③ Drilling → component soldering → power-on testing

[Two Rebuilds]
First failure: residual copper after acid etching caused shorts. After verifying all diodes conducting and transistors operating normally, confirmed the board itself was the issue. Redesigned layout and urgently rebuilt.

Second problem: circuit still non-functional after soldering. Teammate discovered that PCB artwork must be viewed "from below" — all power IC and transistor pins were mirrored. After re-soldering, circuit showed intermittent signal with voltage dropping after 5 seconds. After confirming all solder joint continuity, the circuit operated smoothly.
detail.outcome:
- Circuit successfully drives an 8Ω laboratory speaker with clear audio output
- Completed the full flow: circuit simulation → breadboard verification → PCB fabrication
- Power: 12V DC power supply / 9V battery (portable use)
- Input: 3.5mm stereo cable from computer

[Personal Reflection]
This project gave me a clear understanding of audio amplification principles and deepened my hands-on PCB fabrication skills. The two rebuilds taught me the most — not about design itself, but about systematic debugging: from component-by-component verification to finally identifying the pin mirror issue. Each step required a clear expectation of circuit behavior to eliminate unexpected conditions.

items.name: CE Amplifier Bias Design
items.desc: Calculating R3/R4 voltage divider bias to stabilize Q1 in active region; understanding the tradeoffs between β, DC operating point, and thermal stability in component selection

items.name: CC Emitter Follower
items.desc: Emitter resistor RE introduces negative feedback for low output impedance, reducing loading effects between stages; voltage gain approaches 1

items.name: Bias Stabilization Design
items.desc: D1–D4 in series provide stable voltage difference to Q2/Q3 bases; C3 absorbs turn-off transient reverse voltage; understanding how diode forward voltage temperature characteristics affect bias stability

items.name: Variable Gain Adjustment
items.desc: Rv1a/Rv1b control input signal strength; Rv2 fine-tunes DC operating point; experimentally finding the maximum linear gain boundary without distortion

items.name: Layout Routing Design
items.desc: Hand-drawn double-sided PCB; routing under components to reduce white-line crossings; completed full exposure→development→acid etching process

items.name: Systematic Circuit Debugging
items.desc: Two rebuilds established a debugging flow from component-by-component verification (continuity, saturation, charge) to board-layer short detection; identified residual-copper shorts and pin mirror errors as root causes

items.name: Simulation vs. Real-World Comparison
items.desc: SPICE simulation of time-domain waveform and Bode plot frequency response, then oscilloscope measurement of actual output; analyzing deviations caused by non-ideal component characteristics

projects.title: PCB Trace Defect Detection: Tiled Object Detection with Azure Custom Vision
projects.description: Object detection on the Peking University PCB defect dataset, using 6 board types (01/04/05/06/07/08) and 3 defect classes (missing_hole, mouse_bite, open_circuit). The source images are full 3034×1586 boards where each defect box is only ~60px. Custom Vision shrinks input to ~512px, so defects vanished and the first training run reached only 9.6% mAP / 3.1% recall. After slicing each board into 600px tiles, mAP went up to 88.2% and precision to 100%. The TensorFlow model.pb is exported and loads in Colab.

detail.purpose:
The assignment: from 10 board types, pick at least 3 types and at least 3 defect classes, train an object detector to 80%+ precision, run detection on unseen boards, and export a model.pb that loads in Colab.

The hard part wasn't the training workflow, it was the scale of the data. Each defect box is about 60 pixels while the whole board is 3034×1586. Custom Vision shrinks input to about 512px, leaving defects at ~10px that the model simply can't see. The first run by the standard workflow got 9.6% mAP and 3.1% recall, basically useless.
detail.concept:
[First attempt: upload full images]
Full images with their Pascal VOC annotations, trained on the General (compact) domain with Advanced Training. Result: 9.6% mAP, 3.1% recall, the defects were lost to downscaling.

[Fix: image tiling]
Slice each board into 600×600 tiles centered on each defect box, keeping a box only if at least 40% of its area falls inside the tile, and re-normalizing coordinates to 0–1 within the tile. This raised the defect-to-tile ratio from ~2% to ~12%, so a defect is still ~60px after the 512px resize. The final model trained on 6 board types, 837 tiles across the 3 defects.

[Inference also needs tiling]
A full board gets shrunk at inference too, so PCB_USED and the validation boards are sliced the same way and detected tile by tile, with predictions mapped back to original coordinates and duplicates from overlapping tiles removed by NMS at IoU 0.3.

[Export and Colab]
Exporting TensorFlow requires a compact domain. The exported model outputs a YOLO-style raw tensor (node model_outputs:0); the anchor + NMS decoding reuses Custom Vision's official object_detection.py rather than rewriting it. In Colab, loading that decoder together with model.pb and labels.txt is enough to run inference.
detail.outcome:
[Advanced Training result (6 board types, threshold 0.5)]
Precision 100%　Recall 72.3%　mAP 88.2%
missing_hole　P 100%　R 96.9%　AP 99.6%
mouse_bite　　P 100%　R 65.1%　AP 82.0%
open_circuit　P 100%　R 54.1%　AP 83.0%

[Why training grew from 3 to 6 board types]
The first version trained on only 3 board types (01/04/05) and reached 81.9% mAP, but on unseen boards it added about one extra box per board: it read a normal, drilled via as a missing hole, because it had only seen vias from 3 board types. Adding three more board types (06/07/08) pushed mAP to 88.2% and removed those false positives.

[Validation on board types the model never saw (09–12)]
Training used 6 board types. Validation used 106 defect images from board types 09, 10, 11 and 12: 538 ground-truth defects in total against 509 predicted boxes, now slightly conservative (about 5% under) instead of over-predicting. Detecting defects steadily on unseen board types means the generalization is real.

[Honest limit]
The 100% precision is still Custom Vision's cross-validation number and is optimistic, since tiles from the same board share an almost identical background and inflate it. Among the three classes, open_circuit and mouse_bite have lower recall (54% and 65%): a broken trace and a mouse bite are subtler and more irregular than a missing hole, so some are missed, and the next step would be adding data for those two. missing_hole is already solid (96.9% recall).

items.name: Image Tiling
items.desc: Slice 600px tiles centered on each defect box, keep a box only if ≥40% of its area is inside the tile, re-normalize to 0–1; defect ratio rises from ~2% to ~12%, the make-or-break step of the project

items.name: Pascal VOC Parsing
items.desc: Parse bndbox with xml.etree.ElementTree and convert pixel coordinates to the 0–1 normalized form Custom Vision requires

items.name: Azure Custom Vision SDK
items.desc: Full flow via the Python SDK: create project (compact domain), batch-upload images with regions, Advanced Training, publish, export TensorFlow; Advanced was chosen over Quick Train on purpose

items.name: model.pb Export and Colab Loading
items.desc: Export the frozen graph model.pb (input Placeholder:0, output model_outputs:0), load it in Colab with tf.compat.v1; the YOLO-style anchor/NMS decoding reuses the official object_detection.py

items.name: Tiled Inference + NMS
items.desc: Detect a full board tile by tile, map predictions back to the original image, then drop duplicate boxes from overlapping tiles with NMS at IoU 0.3

items.name: Held-out Validation
items.desc: Generalization test on 106 images from board types 09–12 that never appeared in training, drawing green (ground truth) and red (prediction) boxes; 538 ground-truth defects against 509 predicted boxes, validating performance on new boards rather than trusting the cross-validation numbers alone

projects.title: Open-Source Hardware Integration: QMK × STM32 Numpad
projects.description: Integrated an open-source PCB layout with the QMK/ChibiOS firmware framework on STM32F072. Real engineering contribution: identified compilation and timing conflicts between the open-source framework and the hardware, reverse-engineered ChibiOS HAL triple-config (chconf/halconf/mcuconf) with LLM-assisted debugging, and restructured configuration files to achieve 100% hardware functionality — USB HID stable, RGB Matrix operational, VIA/VIAL live keymap verified.

images.caption: Completed numpad — top view
In use alongside main keyboard

detail.purpose:
Most commercial USB numpads run closed firmware with no way to customize key logic, lighting effects, or layer switching. For those entering the QMK ecosystem, most tutorials target Pro Micro (ATmega32U4), with very little documentation on ChibiOS HAL configuration for STM32 targets.

Project scope: This project integrates an existing open-source keyboard PCB layout (adapted in EasyEDA Pro for LCSC ordering) with the QMK open-source firmware framework — it does not design hardware from scratch. The primary engineering challenge was diagnosing and resolving compilation failures and hardware timing conflicts that prevented the open-source QMK/ChibiOS stack from running on the STM32F072 target. LLM-assisted debugging tools were used to reverse-engineer the correct ChibiOS HAL configuration.

This project aimed to complete the full flow from blank PCB to a working USB HID keyboard, producing a reproducible open-source integration record — including EasyEDA Pro project files, QMK firmware source, and ChibiOS triple-config documentation.

Specific verification goals:
- Full PCB design and LCSC board ordering flow
- STM32F072 DFU flashing flow (BOOT0 pin control, no programmer needed)
- QMK + ChibiOS HAL triple config (chconf / halconf / mcuconf)
- WS2812B RGB Matrix animation integration and overcurrent protection
- VIA / VIAL GUI key remapping support
detail.concept:
[Core Technology Decisions]
- STM32F072 vs. Arduino Pro Micro (ATmega32U4):
  F072 has built-in USB FS peripheral and enters DFU mode via BOOT0 pin — one USB cable handles both flashing and operation, no ISP programmer required. Pro Micro is more beginner-friendly but its 32KB Flash limits advanced QMK features (RGB Matrix, VIAL).

- ChibiOS vs. Bare-metal HAL:
  QMK Firmware officially uses ChibiOS as an RTOS abstraction layer for non-Arduino targets (STM32 series), ensuring USB HID scan timing portability across MCUs. Bare-metal HAL is lighter but requires manually handling USB interrupt priority and GPIO scan conflicts.

- EasyEDA Pro vs. KiCad:
  LCEDA Pro is deeply integrated with LCSC — component selection, DRC, and board ordering all in one platform, significantly faster iteration for a first PCB project.

[Hardware Architecture]
- MCU: STM32F072 (built-in USB FS, DFU bootloader, no programmer needed)
- PCB designed in EasyEDA Pro (LCEDA), full project files uploaded
- Standard keyboard matrix design to minimize IO usage
- WS2812B addressable RGB for per-key independent lighting
- 100nF decoupling capacitors for power stability

[Firmware Architecture — QMK + ChibiOS]
- chconf.h: ChibiOS kernel thread and tick configuration
- halconf.h: enable required peripherals (USB, GPIO, SPI)
- mcuconf.h: STM32 clock, PLL, peripheral clock source settings
- Custom LAYOUT_numpad_17 mapping to PCB physical layout
- Module separation: w17.h (layout) / w17.c (RGB Matrix init) / config.h (hardware params) / keymap.c (key logic)

[Layer Design]
- Layer 0: Standard numpad input
- Layer 1: RGB Matrix control (RM_NEXT/PREV, Hue/Sat/Val/Speed)
- LT() for "hold to switch layer, tap to input"

[RGB Design]
- RGB_MATRIX (not legacy RGBLIGHT) for animation mode switching
- RGB_MATRIX_MAXIMUM_BRIGHTNESS 120 to prevent overcurrent and overheating

[VIA / VIAL Support]
- Users can remap keys and adjust layers in real time without recompiling
detail.outcome:
- Completed full 17-key USB HID keyboard flow: EasyEDA Pro PCB design → LCSC board ordering → hand-soldering → STM32 DFU flashing → QMK firmware integration
- ChibiOS HAL triple config (chconf/halconf/mcuconf) correctly configured; USB HID recognized stably, key response latency measured < 5ms
- RGB Matrix animation modes all working; RGB_MATRIX_MAXIMUM_BRIGHTNESS 120 effectively prevents LED string overcurrent
- VIA/VIAL GUI remapping verified; layer switching and RGB control adjustable in real time without recompiling
- Identified and fixed PCB pin mirror issue (EasyEDA footprint default Top/Bottom layer orientation difference) — reusable checklist for future PCB designs
- Full source code open-sourced on GitHub: EasyEDA project, QMK firmware, ChibiOS configuration notes

items.name: HAL Peripheral Configuration
items.desc: Reverse-engineered and restructured ChibiOS chconf/halconf/mcuconf triple config using LLM-assisted debugging to resolve compilation failures and timing conflicts between the open-source QMK framework and STM32F072 hardware; correctly set USB, GPIO clock sources, and PLL; understood RTOS tick and peripheral initialization ordering dependencies

items.name: Keyboard Matrix Scanning
items.desc: Designed 5×4 ROW2COL matrix with 5ms debounce supporting 17 keys on minimal IO; understood ghost key causes and diode protection principles

items.name: RGB Matrix Driver
items.desc: Integrated WS2812B per-key addressing; Hue/Sat/Val 3D color control; set max brightness 120 to prevent USB overcurrent; understood animation mode switching and SPI timing

items.name: Layer & Tap-Hold
items.desc: LT() for hold-to-layer, tap-to-input; understanding how QMK tap-hold decision window (tapping_term) affects typing feel and tuning

items.name: MCU Peripheral Circuit
items.desc: STM32 USB FS circuit, 100nF decoupling capacitor layout, and DFU bootloader pin configuration ensuring reliable programmer-free USB flashing path; implemented in EasyEDA Pro

projects.title: FRC 7645 — Team Website & CMS
projects.description: 35% codebase contributor. Senior engineer (65%) built the core React/Firebase/Vite architecture, Auth system, Three.js 3D exhibition hall, and Firestore security rules. My scope: homepage redesign, end-to-end messaging system (Contact form → Firestore → CMS tab), team division identity system, Code Splitting (JS bundle 2.7MB → 820KB), Cloudflare Pages deployment, and TBA API 30-day cache.

detail.purpose:
FRC 7645 (Nangang VHS) needed a public team website and a CMS for non-technical members to manage match schedules, rosters, news, and sponsor content.

Codebase ownership breakdown:
- Senior engineer (65%): Core React / Firebase / Vite architecture and routing, Firebase Auth and i18n, TipTap rich text editor with image compression upload, Three.js 3D exhibition hall (.glb explode animation), Firestore security rules, design system (CSS variables, bento grid, carousel), multi-page setup (Blog, Robots, Sponsors, About, Profile), SEO (sitemap, JSON-LD).
- My contribution (35%): Homepage redesign, messaging system, division identity system, performance optimization, Cloudflare Pages deployment, TBA API cache, CMS restructure, CLAUDE.md workspace memory framework.

This document describes my 35% contribution scope.
detail.concept:
[1. Performance — Code Splitting & Lazy Loading]
Inherited a 2.7MB monolithic JS bundle causing slow initial loads. Implemented route-level chunk splitting via Vite dynamic imports, paired with React.lazy + Suspense for on-demand loading per route. Result: bundle reduced from 2.7MB to 820KB (~70% reduction), with measurable FCP improvement.

[2. TBA API Cache — 30-day localStorage Layer]
TBA API rate-limits under competition-day traffic spikes, causing blank match data pages for visitors. Built a 30-day localStorage cache keyed by endpoint + query string. Each request validates cache freshness; stale or missing entries trigger a fresh fetch and re-cache. Eliminated redundant requests and rate-limit risk on competition days.

[3. Messaging System — End-to-End]
Contact page form writes submissions to a Firestore `messages` collection. Added a CMS MessagesPanel Tab so admins can view and delete messages directly in the dashboard without opening Firebase Console. Both the frontend form and the admin interface are new additions this cycle.

Also wrote the Firestore security rule for the `messages` collection: `allow create: if true` (unauthenticated visitors can submit); `allow read, delete` scoped to admin / teacher only (CMS access); `allow update: if false` (prevents tampering). Without this rule, Firebase's default deny-all would reject any unauthenticated write.

[4. Division Identity System]
Admin multi-select pill UI lets admins assign team members to functional divisions (mechanical, electrical, programming, etc.). Profile schema gained a `showInDivision` field; TeamGroup component auto-filters and renders the correct members from this field, replacing manually maintained static roster lists.

[5. Homepage Redesign]
Replaced marquee-based sponsor scroll with CSS keyframe-animated floating bubble cards, eliminating scroll jank. Added team introduction section and stats block (seasons, awards). Introduced TeamTimeline.jsx (renders milestone timeline from a data array, fully decoupled from layout), Competition.jsx (TBA cache–powered match card handling loading / error / empty states independently), and MessagesPanel.

[6. CI/CD Deployment — Cloudflare Pages]
Connected GitHub repo with PR preview deployments and production deploys on main merge. Configured nkhs.team7645.com custom domain routing and Cloudflare edge caching; cached routes achieve sub-100ms TTFB.

[7. CMS Restructure]
Consolidated fragmented CMS tabs: Carousel + Cards → Homepage Management; Contact + Socials → single module. Restored the Advisor Tab dropped in a prior refactor. Added author/division split fields and the MessagesPanel management tab.
detail.outcome:
- Code Splitting reduced JS bundle from 2.7MB to 820KB; FCP improved measurably
- Messaging system consolidated contact submissions into CMS, eliminating manual forwarding
- TBA API cache eliminated rate-limit incidents on competition days
- nkhs.team7645.com has had zero downtime since deployment; cached routes at sub-100ms TTFB
- Division identity system replaced static roster maintenance with Admin UI operations

projects.title: Wireless Whack-a-Mole (Special Ed Aid)
projects.description: Commissioned by Nangang VHS special education dept: ESP32 + ESP-NOW links one master to four slave units with transistor-relay drive circuits and 3D-printed enclosures — a wireless interactive aid for special-needs students. Received official letter of appreciation.

detail.purpose:
Commissioned project for Nangang VHS special education department to design interactive teaching aids usable both indoors and outdoors for special-needs students. Key requirements:

- Small quantity, simple structure, easy maintenance and storage
- Intuitive operation suitable for special-needs students
- No dependence on power outlets for outdoor use

After referencing basketball machines and air hockey tables, the whack-a-mole format was chosen with wireless communication for outdoor use.
detail.concept:
[System Architecture: ESP-NOW Peer-to-Peer Communication]
ESP32 + ESP-NOW protocol chosen:
- No WiFi/router needed — direct peer-to-peer connection
- Auto-reconnects after power loss or restart, no operation required
- One master + four slaves: master randomly selects a number and broadcasts; selected slave lights up; player presses button, slave sends response signal back

[Circuit Design]
Power: 11.1V (3× 18650 in series) → 7805 step-down to 5V for ESP32 → ESP32 GPIO output → 9013 transistor amplification → relay switches 11.1V to LED

Key component considerations:
- Flyback diode (1N4001) across relay coil absorbs back-EMF to prevent arcing damage
- Transistor amplification solves ESP32 GPIO current insufficient to directly drive relay
- 104 ceramic capacitors at 7805 input/output for decoupling and voltage stability

[Program Logic]
Master: randomly generates 1–4 and broadcasts → enters 200-loop waiting cycle (~10 sec) → on response broadcast 0 to reset and enter next round; timeout advances to next round automatically.
Slave: receives number, compares to self ID to decide whether to light → detects button while lit → sends 1 on press → 0.1s delay then sends 0 to reset (prevents master data stuck at 1)

[Mechanical Design]
- Top cover: 3D printed (v1) → wood + screws (improved v2) for easy replacement
- Enclosure: aluminum square tube + top cover; 3.5° outward-tilted screw holes for stronger attachment
- Bottom cover: strong magnet attachment — tool-free battery access
- Core design philosophy: simplify, avoid over-engineering, prioritize quick repair
detail.outcome:
[Functional Results]
- All 5 units (1 master + 4 slaves) fully operational, usable indoors and outdoors
- Wireless communication stable, auto-reconnects after power loss
- Intuitive operation meeting special education requirements
- Received official letter of appreciation from special education department

[Technical Breakthroughs]
- First use of ESP32 + ESP-NOW for multi-unit communication
- Diagnosed 7805 overheating and ESP32 unexplained failure — understood the practical necessity of decoupling capacitors and flyback diodes
- Advanced from single-unit to multi-unit coordinated control

[Reflection]
Building aids for special-needs students requires additional considerations — safety against injury, easy part replacement, simplicity. The circuit troubleshooting of 7805 overheating and ESP32 failure taught more practical knowledge than the design phase.

[Division of Work — 2-person project]
- Mechanical design and fabrication: Chiang Jia-Yuan
- Circuit design, fabrication, programming: Chiang Jia-Yuan + Du Chuan-You

projects.title: Auto Sanitizer (MoE Science Outreach)
projects.description: Invited to the 2021 Ministry of Education Science Outreach Train: Arduino + HC-SR04 ultrasonic sensor–triggered automatic alcohol dispenser, iterated through a desktop demo version and an FRC robot–integrated version.

detail.purpose:
During the pandemic, invited as an FRC team member from Nangang VHS to participate in the 2021 Ministry of Education Science Outreach Train — touring high schools across Taiwan to showcase technology projects.

Chose to build an automatic touch-free alcohol dispenser: both practically useful for pandemic prevention and a demonstration of ultrasonic sensing, relay circuits, and embedded control for on-site students.

Two iterated versions:
- Desktop version: standalone exhibition unit with acrylic laser-cut enclosure, portable for school tours
- Robot-integrated version: miniaturized circuit integrated into FRC robot side panel for use at competition venue entrances without occupying extra space
detail.concept:
[Sensing & Trigger Logic]
HC-SR04 continuously measures forward distance; when a hand enters the trigger zone (~10–15 cm), Arduino UNO outputs HIGH to relay module. Relay closes, driving the water pump to extract alcohol from the reservoir through the nozzle. Motor stops immediately when hand withdraws.

[Power Design]
2× 18650 lithium batteries in series provide 7.6V; 7805 step-down module to 5V for Arduino; motor draws directly from battery; relay isolates control from high-current drive.

[Mechanical Design]
- v2.0: Acrylic laser-cut panels with interlocking tabs for clean appearance and easy alcohol refilling
- v3.0: Miniaturized circuit board integrated into FRC robot side panel; adjustable nozzle height for different user heights
detail.outcome:
- Successfully completed 2021 MoE Science Outreach Train, presenting to students at multiple high schools
- Iterated from desktop version to robot-integrated version, learning "design optimization per use case" product thinking
- Technical takeaways: ultrasonic ranging principles, relay drive circuits, 7805 power regulation and decoupling capacitor selection

projects.title: Team-Built Competition Robot
projects.description: Represented Nangang VHS in the 52nd National Skills Competition (Team Project category): three-member team jointly designed a pick-and-place robot driven by a 24V lead-acid battery with relay H-bridge motor control, capable of handling both cylindrical and rectangular objects.

detail.purpose:
Represented Nangang VHS in the 52nd National Skills Competition (2022), Team Project category. Rules required a three-person team to jointly design and build a functional robot within a time limit — goal: move across the field and pick and place cylindrical and rectangular objects.

The competition emphasizes cross-disciplinary collaboration: mechanical design, circuit wiring, and programming control must be tightly integrated to complete an effective robot within the competition time constraint.
detail.concept:
[Chassis & Drive]
Rubber wheel chassis driven by a 24V lead-acid battery pack. Relay bank implements H-bridge circuit — two relays per drive axis switch motor current direction to control forward/reverse. Direct, reliable circuit structure.

[Gripper Mechanism]
Gripper designed to handle both cylinders and rectangular objects simultaneously: curved inner surface conforms to cylinders, flat surface stably grips rectangular objects — ensuring no slippage during transport.

[Three-Person Division of Work]
- Mechanical design and fabrication (Chiang Jia-Yuan): chassis structure, gripper design, and all mechanical part machining
- Circuit design and wiring (team members): H-bridge relay drive circuit, 24V system wiring
- Programming and control logic (team members): motor direction control program
detail.outcome:
- Represented Nangang VHS and completed the 52nd National Skills Competition, Team Project category
- Technical takeaways: high-power relay H-bridge circuit design, 24V system wiring standards, multi-person synchronized development
- Understanding the difference between competition robot development and FRC's longer development cycle — functional integration under extreme time constraints

items.desc: Curved gripping surface for cylinders, flat surface for rectangular objects — accommodating two cross-sections without tool changes; understanding geometric constraints for gripping stability

projects.title: Swerve Drive Omnidirectional Research
projects.description: Self-developed three generations of FRC swerve drive chassis from scratch, covering kinematics algorithms, gear transmission design, CNC precision machining, and LabVIEW PID control. Earned 2022 FRC Taiwan Innovation in Control Award.

detail.purpose:
Top international FRC teams widely use Swerve Drive — its four-wheel independent steering architecture provides omnidirectional movement advantages while solving mecanum wheels' traction deficiency and vulnerability to impacts. However, virtually no domestic research material existed in Taiwan, and no standardized Chinese terminology had been established.

The team decided to develop from scratch, iterating through three generations (2020, 2022, 2023+), with research goals covering:

① Achieving all motion modes of an omnidirectional mobile platform through mechanical structure
② Improving robot operational efficiency and maneuverability in competition
③ Third generation: lightweight design as core direction
④ Modular aluminum plate design: drive and steering motor positions interchangeable; drive reduction ratio adjustable by swapping gears — reducing assembly and maintenance complexity
⑤ Achieve fully self-designed, self-machined goals
⑥ Exchange mechanism technology with international teams at competition venues
detail.concept:
[Three-Generation Evolution]
- Gen 1 (2020): Designed by senior members, used at CSIA 5G Regional — validated basic Swerve Drive feasibility
- Gen 2 (2022): Redesigned and competed, earned Taiwan Foxconn Regional Innovation in Control Award
- Gen 3 (2022+): Continued refinement with lightweight and modular design as core, preparing for next season

[Kinematics Algorithm — LabVIEW Implementation]
Representing wheel direction and force as vector X∠θ, decomposing motion into three additive modes:
- Translation: all 4 wheels input same angle and force, no body rotation
- Rotation: center at all-wheel intersection, wheels at θ, -θ, 180+θ, 180-θ — rotate in place
- Arc: translation vector + rotation vector = arbitrary-center arc trajectory
Normalize by the maximum-force wheel as denominator to ensure output doesn't exceed motor saturation.

[Electrical & Control System]
- Drive motors: Falcon 500 (integrated Talon FX controller with closed-loop speed control)
- Steering motors: NEO 550 (with planetary gearbox)
- Rotary encoders: per-wheel independent angle feedback for closed-loop steering control
- Main controller: roboRIO + LabVIEW graphical programming

[Reduction Ratio Design]
- Drive: Falcon 500 (6380 rpm) → 1:7 → 911 rpm, approximately 110 m/min linear speed
- Steering: NEO 550 (11000 rpm) → planetary 1:28.1 → gear 1:2 → total 1:56.2 → 196 rpm

[Gen 3 Mechanical Design]
- Modular aluminum plates: drive and steering motor mounting holes correspond and are interchangeable; drive gear zone has swappable gear positions for reduction ratio adjustment without modifying aluminum plates
- Lightweight: most internal gears switched to 3D printing (FDM), retaining only motor shaft gears as metal; aluminum plate CNC material removal for weight reduction
- Collision protection: motors fully enclosed in mechanism interior, reducing competition collision damage
- Custom wheels: replaced OEM wheels, improved traction

[Gear Specs & Machining]
- M1.25/T15 spur gears (drive motor shaft): wire EDM for profile and bore
- M1.5/T24 spur gears (steering transmission): wire EDM
- M1.5/T15 bevel gears (wheel drive direction change): wire EDM for bore
- Transmission shafts (hex & square cross-sections): CNC mill + 4-jaw precision chuck, two-pass bilateral milling for symmetry
- Wire EDM workflow: clamp plate → edge-finding XY correction → thread wire → path simulation → cut → inspect cross-section for accuracy
detail.outcome:
[Competition Results]
- 2022 FRC Taiwan Foxconn Regional: Finalist Alliance + Innovation in Control Award
- 2020 FRC CSIA 5G Digital Regional: Best Industrial Design Award

[Gen 3 vs Gen 2 Improvements]
Structural:
① Lightweight: most gears switched to 3D printing, significant overall weight reduction
② Disassembly: reduced complexity, faster repair and replacement
③ Modular: drive/steering motor holes shared, compatible with NEO or Falcon 500
④ Collision protection: motors fully enclosed, reduced field collision damage risk
⑤ Wheels: custom design, improved traction over old wheels

Power:
① Drive gear tooth count swappable — reduction ratio adjustable without modifying aluminum plates
② NEO upgraded to Falcon 500, approximately 1000 rpm speed increase

[Personal Takeaways]
- Design→Build→Test→Iterate complete cycle from a product development perspective
- Machining skills advanced from manual mill/lathe basics to CNC and wire EDM
- Cross-domain integration: mechanical design (Inventor) + electrical control + LabVIEW programming + competition field engineering decisions
